sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: As generative AI is increasingly applied to automate multi-step and high-stake workflows, human judgment and involvement remain essential for ensuring the quality of AI-generated outputs. In practice, while it is desirable for human experts to provide oversight on AI regularly, often by reviewing intermediate outputs, giving feedback, making corrections, and steering subsequent steps, such oversight is constrained by the time and resources that humans can afford. This creates a tension between the need for human oversight and AI's efficiency in delivering more output with less intervention. An important but underexplored question, then, is how to optimally engage humans in human-AI coworking. This work was originally motivated by our empirical observation that in long AI workflows, human oversight often improves user satisfaction while reducing unnecessary rework and token consumption. From there, we formulate the problem of where to place oversight stages in human-AI coworking. Under reasonable assumptions, we then develop the nonuniformity principle, which states that the optimal schedule places oversight stages with non-decreasing gaps along the workflow. We empirically validate this principle in two common AI agent workflows: writing literature reviews and constructing websites.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: Nonuniformity Principle in Human-AI Coworking An Luo and Jie Ding School of Statistics, University of Minnesota luo00318@umn.edu and dingj@umn.edu Abstract As generative AI is increasingly applied to automate multi-step and high-stake workflows, human judgment and involvement remain essential for ensuring the quality of AI-generated outputs. In practice, while it is desirable for human experts to provide oversight on AI regularly, often by reviewing intermediate outputs, giving feedback, making corrections, and steering subsequent steps, such oversight is constrained by the time and resources that humans can afford. This creates tension between the need for human oversight and AIs efficiency in delivering more output with less intervention. An important but underexplored question, then, is how to optimally engage humans in human-AI coworking. This work was originally motivated by our empirical observation that in long AI workflows, human oversight often improves user satisfaction while reducing unnecessary rework and token consumption. From there, we formulate the problem of where to place oversight stages in human-AI coworking. Under reasonable assumptions, we then develop the nonuniformity principle, which states that the optimal schedule places oversight stages with non-decreasing gaps along the workflow. We empirically validate this principle in two common AI agent workflows: writing literature reviews and constructing websites. Keywords: Human-AI Coworking, AI Auditing, Agentic AI, Scalable Oversight

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Generative AI is increasingly moving from single-shot generation based on large language models (LLMs) [Wei et al., 2022, Ouyang et al., 2022, OpenAI et al., 2023, Gemini Team Google, 2023] toward long-horizon workflows, such as resolving real-world software engineering issues [Jimenez et al., 2024], navigating the web to accomplish user-specified goals [He et al., 2024], and producing extended written reports [Wang et al., 2024]. In such long-horizon workflows, AI needs to work over multiple steps [Yao et al., 2023], use external tools [Qin et al., 2024, Patil et al., 2024], and coordinate different operations [Hong et al., 2024, Wu et al., 2024]. It remains critical, however, to keep human oversight in the loop. For example, when AI is used to automate drug discovery [Koscher et al., 2023, Abramson et al., 2024, DeMeo et al., 2025], human experts still need to engage at multiple stages of the workflow, such as refining the biological objective, assessing whether proposed candidates are scientifically meaningful, and deciding which ones should be further experimentally validated. When AI automates laboratory operation [Boiko et al., 2023, Szymanski et al., 2023, Dai et al., 2024], humans still need to provide oversight at multiple stages, such as specifying experimental constraints, monitoring safety, and judging whether the measurements support the intended claim. In practice, while it is desirable for human experts to provide oversight on AI regularly to ensure the quality of its output, such oversight is constrained by the time and resources that humans can afford. This creates tension between the need for human oversight and AIs efficiency in delivering more output with less intervention. An important but underexplored question, then, is how to optimally engage humans in human-AI coworking. Existing research provides limited guidance on this question. Much work has studied how humans should provide feedback to AI systems [Amershi et al., 2019, Ouyang et al., 2022], while growing literature examines the benefits of human involvement in complex domain-specific tasks, including medical decision-making [Reverberi et al., 2022, Vaccaro et al., 2024, Wang et al., 2026], scientific writing [Gero et al., 2022, Liang et al., 2024, Thakkar et al., 2026], and data science [Meng, 2023, Luo et al., 2025a,b, 2026]. Much less is known, however, about how human oversight with limited number of human oversight stages should be scheduled within long-horizon workflow of AI. Our investigation is motivated by the empirical observation that in long AI workflows, human oversight often improves user satisfaction while reducing unnecessary rework and token consumption. From there, we formulate the problem of where to place the oversight stages in human-AI coworking. Under reasonable assumptions, we then develop the nonuniformity principle, which states that the optimal schedule places oversight stages with non-decreasing gaps along the workflow. Figure 1 gives an illustration of the nonuniformity principle. Figure 1: Illustration of the nonuniformity principle. (a) Different oversight schedules with the same number of oversight stages. The green star schedule places oversight relatively densely near the beginning and then uses increasing gaps between later oversight stages, which follows the nonuniformity principle. The yellow triangle schedule uses uniform gaps, and the red square schedule uses decreasing gaps. (b) Quality-cost trade-off for these schedules. Here, cost stands for the human oversight cost. The schedule under the nonuniformity principle is optimal among all four schedules. The uniform and decreasing-gap schedules require larger cost without improving quality. The gray circle represents no oversight, which has the lowest cost but also the lowest quality. We first formulate the problem of human-AI coworking, where an AI agent builds deliverable 3 step by step while the humans intention of what the deliverable should ultimately satisfy remains hidden from the agent. The agent only starts with an initial context and must produce the final deliverable in stages. At selected stages, the human provides oversight based on the underlying intention and what the agent already produced, and the agent can revise the deliverable produced so far based on the human input. An oversight cost is incurred by the human in these stages. For fixed number of oversight stages, the goal is to optimize the schedule of the oversight stages = {s1, . . . , sK} to balance two forces: the quality of alignment between the final deliverable produced by the agent and the human intention, and the human oversight cost. Building upon our formulation of human-AI coworking, the key idea behind our theory is to measure what happens between two consecutive oversight stages. We assume that after the human provides oversight, the agent is better aligned with the human intention. As the agent then works on its own for more stages, its uncertainty about the human intention can grow, so the expected alignment error is assumed to increase with the number of stages since the last oversight. Under reasonable assumptions, we will show that the original scheduling problem can be reduced to much simpler form, which pertains to scheduling between the oversight stages. And we further develop the nonuniformity principle that the optimal oversight schedules have non-decreasing gaps. Here, gap means the number of production stages between neighboring human oversight. The resulting schedule uses oversight more frequently early on. Intuition is that, at early stages, human oversight can quickly narrow the AIs long-term search space to align with humans unobserved intent. Later in the process, oversight becomes more costly but is still necessary to continue to refine the work to deliver high-quality final result. We demonstrate the practical value of the nonuniformity principle through experiments on two common long-horizon tasks: writing literature reviews and constructing HTML pages. 4 The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 formalizes the problem of humanAI coworking. Section 3 develops the nonuniformity principle and provides practical guide to find the optimal oversight schedule. Section 4 presents experimental results and examines their agreement with the theory. We conclude this paper in Section 5. Supplementary material includes proofs and details of discussions and experiments.

sections.title: 2 Problem Formulation of Human-AI Coworking
sections.content: We begin with description of the human-AI coworking problem. human has an intended deliverable in mind, but this intent is only partially available to the AI agent through the initial context. Starting from this initial context, the agent constructs the deliverable over sequential stages, producing one component at time. At selected stages, the human reviews the partial deliverable produced so far and provides oversight. Such oversight can help revise previously produced content, clarify the humans intent, and guide the agents future production. At such oversight stages, the agent revises the working deliverable and then proceeds. The final deliverable is evaluated by how well each stage-level output aligns with the corresponding latent requirement implied by the humans intention. Each oversight also incurs human oversight cost, as the human must spend time and effort inspecting the current draft before giving feedback. The goal is to schedule fixed number of oversight stages so that the final deliverable has high alignment quality and the human oversight cost remains low. An overview of main concepts in the formulation of human-AI coworking is given in Figure 2. In this paper, an AI agent, or simply an agent, refers to system that integrates data, tools, memory, operations, and human feedback to continuously generate actions [Tian et al., 2025]. We consider an AI agent that carries out the work through up to production stages. If k=1 Figure 2: Overview of main concepts in human-AI coworking. The AI agent constructs deliverable sequentially from stage 1 to stage . At each oversight stage sk in the schedule = {sk}K , with 1 s1 < s2 < < sK , the human provides oversight based on the specification θ, incurring oversight cost c(sk). Red blocks denote newly produced but not yet , while green blocks denote components revised after human oversight reviewed components at sk, such as w[sk]+ . Thus, between two consecutive oversight stages, the agent continues 1:sk producing unreviewed components, and at the next oversight the human reviews the current deliverable according to θ, after which the agent revises the reviewed components and continues are produced production. After the final oversight stage sK, the remaining components without further review. The resulting final deliverable under schedule is denoted by ew1:T (S). The same specification θ also determines the stage-level requirements Z1:T , against which the final deliverable is evaluated. The scheduling objective is to choose to balance final alignment loss and human oversight cost: J(S) = EhPT k=1 c(sk), subject to 1 s1 < s2 < < sK . t=1 ewt(S) Zt2i+PK sK +1:T 6 task has fewer than stages, one may add dummy stages that produce no new substantive content. Without loss of generality, we suppose the agents work consists of production stages. In some tasks, the stages are natural production units. For example, writing paper may be organized into = 6 parts, such as abstract, introduction, related work, method, experiments, and conclusion. In some other tasks, the stages may be milestones in pipeline. For example, data analysis task may proceed through stages such as data cleaning, exploratory analysis, model fitting, validation, and report writing. We suppose the human has an intended deliverable in mind when coworking with AI. We denote this intended deliverable by specification θ Θ, where Θ is the space of possible specifications. The specification θ determines what the human would regard as correct, complete, and well aligned with the task. The AI agent does not observe θ, as the full specification may be highly dependent on domain knowledge and too costly to communicate before production begins. For example, in scientific writing, Θ may include the intended argument, the relevant literature, the desired level of technical detail, and the authors judgment about what should be emphasized. Such information can be costly to write down in full and may involve domain knowledge that is difficult for the agent to infer from the initial description alone. What is available to the AI agent is an initial context D, where is the space of possible initial contexts. is general set that can include the task description, examples, available tools, data sources, reference materials, and other resources that the agent can use when producing the deliverable. At each stage, there is corresponding target requirement implied by θ. It is what the current component should accomplish in order for the final deliverable to match the humans intent. For example, in paper writing, the introduction should motivate the problem, the related work should position the paper against prior studies, and the method section should explain the proposed approach. Let Zt denote the requirement at stage t, where is the requirement space. For technical simplicity, we set = W, treating the requirement at each stage as the ideal deliverable for that stage. Let Z1:T = (Z1, . . . , ZT ) denote the full sequence of stage-level requirements. We also introduce Z0, latent initial state representing the requirement before production begins. Let Qθ,0 denote the distribution of Z0. Conditional on θ, we model Z1:T as general conditional process, Zt (Z0:t1 = z0:t1, θ) Qθ( z0:t1), = 1, . . . , T, (1) where Qθ( z0:t1) is the conditional distribution of Zt given the past requirements z0:t1 and specification θ. At each stage = 1, . . . , , the agent produces draft t W, not yet reviewed. Depending on the task, may be paragraph, code section, table, or another task-specific component. The space of deliverables across all stages is . The human provides oversight at selected stages. Oversight may take different forms: clarifying intent, correcting content, giving feedback on the partial deliverable, or providing task-specific evidence such as test or execution results. An oversight schedule is set := {sk}K k=1 with 1 s1 < < sK and < , since the human cannot review every stage. Each oversight stage incurs cost c(s) 0, reflecting the effort to inspect the partial deliverable at stage and give feedback. At an oversight stage S, the agents deliverable has two parts: the revised deliverable from the last oversight, w[τ (s)]+ 1:τ (s) τ (s) (with w[0]+ τ (s)+1:s sτ (s) produced since then. Here τ (t) := max(cid:16) most recent oversight stage before t; τ (t) = 0 means no prior oversight. In the experimental studies, we will consider general loss functions. Let ew1:T (S) = ( ew1(S), . . . , ewT (S)) denote the final deliverable under oversight schedule S. This is the fully revised deliverable following the procedure above. For each stage t, ewt(S) is given by ewt(S) := w[sK ]+ if sK, if sK < T. (3) 9 Define the expected alignment loss under as L(S) := ewt(S) Zt2 # . " t=1 (4) Define J(S) := L(S) + PK k=1 c(sk) as the total loss, combining alignment loss and oversight cost. The objective is min S={sk}K k=1 J(S) s.t. 1 s1 < < sK T. (5) It aims to find the schedule that best balances alignment quality and oversight cost.

sections.title: 3 The Nonuniformity Principle
sections.content: In this section, we develop the nonuniformity principle. In Section 3.1, we introduce some assumptions. In Section 3.2, we present nonuniformity principle as the main results. In Section 3.3, we provide practical guide for finding the optimal schedule.

sections.title: 3.1 Assumptions and preparations
sections.content: Suppose after an oversight stage S, the agent produces 1 additional drafts s+1, . . . , s+d without yet being reviewed, i.e., τ (s + d) = s. To measure how prediction error accumulates after an oversight, let ρs(r) denote the expected conditional variance of Zs+r given the information available at stage s, i.e., ρs(r) := E[Var(Zs+r Hs)] , = 1, . . . , d. (6) Assumption 1. For each {0} and each integer > 1, Zs+r is conditionally independent of (w s+1, . . . , s+r1 ) given Hs. Assumption 1 decouples the latent requirement process from the agents intermediate deliverables. That is, the future requirement Zs+r depends only on the information available at stage s, not on the drafts produced in between. Lemma 1. Under Assumption 1, for each {0} and each positive integer satisfying τ (s + r) = s, Eh s+r Zs+r2i = ρs(r). (7) Lemma 1 says that the expected squared error of the agents draft at stage + equals ρs(r), the conditional variance of Zs+r as seen from stage s. Assumption 2. There exists function ρ(r) 0 such that ρs(r) = ρ(r) for any {0} and any integer 1 satisfying τ (s + r) = s. Assumption 2 states that, after any oversight, the expected prediction error at lag depends only on r, not on which stage the oversight occurs. Remark 1 (When does Assumption 2 hold?). Suppose the oversight at stage is clear enough to fully resolve the current requirement, meaning Var(Zs Hs) = 0 for each {0}. Then all remaining uncertainty about Zs+r comes from future evolution alone. Under this condition, we give two examples in which Assumption 2 holds. 1) Random walk. Zt+1 = Zt +εt+1 for = 0, . . . , 1, where the innovations ε1, . . . , εT are independent, have mean zero, and have common variance σ2 > 0. Then, for any {0} and any 1 satisfying τ (s + r) = s, we have Zs+r = Zs + Pr q=1 εs+q, and Var(Zs+r Hs) = Pr q=1 Var(εs+q) = σ2r. Therefore ρs(r) = ρ(r) = σ2r. 2) Stable AR(1). Zt+1 = µ + α(Zt µ) + εt+1 for = 0, . . . , 1, where µ is constant, 0 < α < 1, and the innovations ε1, . . . , εT are independent, have mean zero, and have common variance σ2 ε > 0. Then, for any {0} and any 1 satisfying τ (s + r) = s, we have Zs+r = µ + αr(Zs µ) + Pr q=1 αrqεs+q and Var(Zs+r Hs) = σ2 ε Pr q=1 α2(rq) = σ2 ε (1 α2r)/(1 α2). Therefore ρs(r) = ρ(r) = σ2 ε (1 α2r)/(1 α2). In both examples, Assumption 2 holds. Together, Lemma 1 and Assumption 2 establish that E[w s+r Zs+r2] = ρs(r) = ρ(r). With s0 := 0, we impose the following assumption on the final deliverable. Assumption 3. There exists constant κ (0, 1) such that, for every = 0, . . . , 1 and every integer satisfying sk < sk+1, where s1, . . . , sK are the oversight stages in S, E[ ewt(S) Zt2] = κ ρ(t sk). Assumption 3 states that the expected squared error at any stage between two oversights is fixed fraction κ of ρ. This captures the benefit of oversight: reviewed stages have lower error (by factor κ < 1) than they would without it. We can now decompose L(S) in terms of the gaps between oversight stages. By Lemma 1 and Assumption 2, we have E[w s+r Zs+r2] = ρs(r) = ρ(r) for any {0} and for each = 1, . . . , d. By Assumption 3, we have E[ ews+r Zs+r2] = κ ρ(r) for any {0} {sK} and for each = 1, . . . , d. So for any {0} {sK} we have Pd r=1 E[ ews+r Zs+r2] = κ Pd r=1 ρ(r) := Φ(d) for 1, and we set Φ(0) = 0. For = sK we have Pd r=1 E[ ews+r Zs+r2] = Pd r=1 ρ(r) := Ψ(d) for 1, and we set Ψ(0) = 0. Assumption 4. ρ(r) is strictly increasing in r. Remark 2 (When does Assumption 4 hold?). We consider the same two models in Remark 1: 1) Random walk. ρ(r) = σ2r is strictly increasing in r. 2) Stable AR(1). ρ(r) = σ2 ε (1 α2r)/(1 α2) is strictly increasing in r. In both cases, Assumption 4 holds. Assumption 5. The oversight cost c(s) is strictly increasing in s. 12 Lemma 2. Under Assumption 4, Φ(d) and Ψ(d) are strictly increasing and strictly discrete convex in d. Let sK+1 := , and dk := sk+1 sk for = 0, . . . , K. Since L(S) = hPT PT t=1 [ ewt(S) Zt2] = PK k=0 Psk+1 t=sk+ [ ewt(S) Zt2] = PK k=0 Pdk r=1 t=1 ewt(S) Zt2i = [ ewsk+r(S) Zsk+r2] = PK1 j= Φ(dj) + Ψ(dK), the objective of minimizing the loss (4) (but without oversight costs) reduces to min d0,...,dK K1 j=0 Φ(dj) + Ψ(dK) s.t. j=0 dj = T, d0, . . . , dK1 1, dK 0. (8) The following Proposition 1 shows that when the oversight cost is negligible, the optimal schedule spreads nearly uniformly: any two gaps between consecutive oversight stages differ by at most one. Proposition 1. Let d,0 := (d,0 0 , . . . , d,0 ) be any minimizer of (8). Under Assumption 4, max0jK1 d,0 min0jK1 d,0 1.

sections.title: 3.2 Non-decreasing gaps under increasing oversight cost
sections.content: We now consider the objective (5) for quality-cost trade-off. Using the decomposition L(S) = PK1 j=0 Φ(dj) + Ψ(dK), the objective with oversight cost, objective (5), becomes min d0,...,dK K1 j= Φ(dj) + Ψ(dK) + k=1 c(sk) s.t. j=0 dj = T, d0, . . . , dK1 1, dK 0, (9) where sk = Pk1 l=0 dl. Remark 3 (Existence of the optimal schedule). The feasible set in (9) is finite, so global minimizer exists. 13 Theorem 1 (The nonuniformity principle: non-decreasing gaps). Under Assumptions 4 and 5, every minimizer := (d 0, . . . , ) of (9) satisfies 0 1 K1. Theorem 1 formalizes the nonuniformity principle: with fixed number of oversight stages, an optimal schedule places oversight relatively densely early in the process, and the gaps before later oversight stages are no smaller than the earlier ones. Without oversight cost, Proposition 1 shows that the reviewed gaps differ no more than one. The oversight cost breaks this balance. As later oversight stages require reviewing longer deliverable, the optimal schedule shifts oversight stages earlier and produces gaps that are non-decreasing over time. Theorem 1 does not impose result involving . This is because K is the terminal gap after the last oversight stage, rather than gap ending at an oversight stage. In applications, it is appealing to set sK = , equivalently dK = 0, and apply the same scheduling idea to the earlier oversight stages, as this corresponds to final review-and-revision step after the agent has produced the final deliverable. Theorem 2 (The nonuniformity principle: earliest oversight stages under high oversight cost). Under Assumption 4, if min1sT 1{c(s + 1) c(s)} > ρ(T K) κρ(2), then 0 = = K1 = 1 and = K. Theorem 2 states that, if the oversight cost grows fast, the optimal schedule would be to place all oversight stages at the first stages. This means that when it is too costly for the human to provide oversight, the oversight stages should be set as early as possible. Corollary 2.1. Suppose c(s) = λs with λ > 0 in the objective (9). Under Assumption 4, if λ > ρ(T K) κρ(2), then 0 = = K1 = 1 and = K.

sections.title: 3.3 A practical guide for finding the optimal schedule
sections.content: To give an exact algorithm for finding the optimal schedule as practical guide, here we take c(s) = λs, where λ > 0 is constant representing how costly reviewing is to the human. This choice reasonably assumes that reviewing deliverable with units requires effort proportional to the amount of content. Since sk = Pk1 j=0 dj, we have k=1 c(sk) = λ k=1 sk = λ k1 k=1 j=0 dj = λ K1 (K j)dj. j=0 (10) To give practically simple algorithm, here we assume that Zt evolves as the random walk model described in Remark 1. This gives ρ(r) = σ2r, and hence Φ(d) = κσ2 2 d(d + 1) and Ψ(d) = σ 2 d(d + 1). (11) From the objective (9), with the random walk model under the linear oversight cost c(s) = λs, the exact scheduling objective is given by (combining (9), (10), and (11)) Jλ(d0, . . . , dK) = (κσ 2 dj(dj + 1) + λ(K j)dj ) + σ2 2 dK(dK + 1), K1 j= (12) subject to PK j=0 dj = , d0, . . . , dK1 1, and dK 0. Let η := λ/σ2. Dividing (12) by σ2 gives the normalized objective Jκ,η(d0, . . . , dK) = K1 j=0 (cid:26)κ 2 dj(dj + 1) + η(K j)dj (cid:27) + 1 2 dK(dK + 1), (13) subject to PK j=0 dj = , d0, . . . , dK1 1, dK 0, and d0, . . . , dK Z. The objective (13) depends only on two effective parameters: the revision factor κ and the 15 ratio η = λ/σ2. The following algorithm gives an exact schedule that is optimal. Algorithm 1 scheduling guide based on the random walk model with linear cost Require: Number of production stages , number of oversight stages K, revision factor κ (0, 1), ratio η = λ/σ2. Ensure: Gap vector bd = ( bd0, . . . , bdK) and oversight schedule bS = {bs1, . . . , bsK}. 1: Initialize = (d0, . . . , dK1, dK) (1, . . . , 1, 0) ZK+1. 2: Set K. 3: 4: for = 1, . . . , do Compute j(d) κ(dj + 1) + η(K j), dK + 1, = 0, . . . , 1, = K. Select any jb arg minj=0,...,K j(d). + 1. Update djb djb 5: 6: end for 7: 8: Set bd d. 9: Compute bsk Pk1 10: Set bS {bs1, . . . , bsK}. return bd and bS. 11: j=0 bdj, for = 1, . . . , K. Proposition 2. Let T, Z+ with < , κ (0, 1), and η > 0. Then the gap vector bd returned by Algorithm 1 is global minimizer of (13). Algorithm 1 gives direct implementation to find an optimal schedule guaranteed by Proposition 2. The user needs to specify , K, κ, and η = λ/σ2. In practice, could be the number of natural production units, such as paragraphs in writing task, sections in webpage construction task, or modules in coding task. The number of oversight stages is determined by how many times the human is willing or able to provide oversight. The revision factor κ represents how much alignment error remains after human oversight. small value of κ corresponds to highly effective oversight, and κ closer to one corresponds to weaker oversight. Thus, κ (0, 1) can be set based on how effective the user feels about the oversight they would provide. The parameter η = λ/σ2 R+ compares the burden of reviewing longer deliverable with the uncertainty in the agents production. smaller η is appropriate when review is relatively easy, or when the user is more concerned about 16 accumulated uncertainty and therefore willing to review later drafts. larger η is appropriate when reviewing longer drafts is burdensome, or when the user prefers to provide earlier oversight before the deliverable becomes costly to inspect.

sections.title: 4 Experiments
sections.content: In this section, we present our experimental observations that motivate the nonuniformity principle, and also verify that the theoretical results in Section 3 align with the experimental results. We consider two long-horizon tasks where the human specification is often not revealed in full: writing the related work section for research paper, and constructing an HTML page. These two tasks represent two prominent contemporary applications of AI agents: text generation [Lin et al., 2025, Huot et al., 2025] and code generation [Jimenez et al., 2024, Yang et al., 2024, Wang et al., 2025]. The rest of this section is organized as follows: Section 4.1 presents the shared experimental setup for the two tasks. Section 4.2 presents oversight scheduling for writing related work. Section 4.3 presents the same problem in HTML page construction. Section 4.4 connects the empirical observations to the nonuniformity principle and the proposed algorithm in Section 3.

sections.title: 4.1 Setup shared by the two tasks
sections.content: For both tasks, we set = 10 for production stages and = 3 for oversight stages. To examine how different schedules behave in the quality-cost trade-off, we test six candidate schedules: five schedules whose gaps increase, remain flat, or decrease, and one schedule with no oversight serving as baseline. Table 1 presents details of the six schedules considered in our experiments. For each task we measure quality metric with range 110 (higher is better) and cost 17 Table 1: Six oversight schedules considered in experiments (T = 10, = 3 for all except Skip). Oversight Schedule = {sk}3 with 1 s1 < s2 < s3 10. Gaps dj = sj+1 sj with s0 = 0 for = 0, 1, 2. k=1 Name Oversight Schedule Gaps (d0, d1, d2) Gap trend Skip Burst-Early Tilt-Early Spread Uniform Burst-Late {1, 2, 3} {1, 3, 6} {1, 4, 9} {2, 5, 8} {7, 8, 9} (1, 1, 1) (1, 2, 3) (1, 3, 5) (2, 3, 3) (7, 1, 1) flat increasing increasing flat decreasing metric (lower is better) and identify the Pareto hull in the resulting quality-cost space. The objective (5) defined in Section 2 is population expected loss over the latent requirement process. In the experiments, each task provides one specification θ but the requirement process is still not directly available. We therefore estimate schedule-level performance by the empirical average over task instances. We use the quality score given by judge as proxy for the alignment quality, while the review cost is measured directly from the length of the working deliverable. Details of how such quality score is obtained are given in Sections 4.2 and 4.3, respectively. For each schedule S, we report bQ(S), the average of the quality score over tasks, and bC(S), the average of the cost over tasks. We use the loss Lλ(S) = 10 bQ(S) + λ bC(S) as proxy of the objective (5) defined in Section 2. In this section, schedule is on the Pareto hull if it minimizes Lλ(S) for some λ 0 among the candidate oversight schedules with = 3.

sections.title: 4.2 Study 1: writing related work
sections.content: This study focuses on human-AI coworking for writing the related work section in scientific paper. We present the setup, metrics, and results below, and the full implementation details of this study are in Section of the supplementary material. 18 Setup. We sample = 40 accepted papers from International Conference on Learning Representations [2026] stratified across 18 primary areas. Related work sections in the papers sampled range from 203 to 658 words (median 281). For each paper as task instance, we set up three roles, agent for writing related work, human for providing oversight, and judge for giving quality scores on the final deliverables, each realized with separate LLM calls with temperature = 0: Agent (writing related work, deepseek-v4-flash): given only the paper title and abstract, writes one paragraph of related work per step = 1, . . . , 10. Rewrite the deliverable at any oversight stage based on human oversight. Human (providing oversight, deepseek-v4-pro): at any oversight stage S, given the real related work section in the paper as specification θ and related work written by the agent up to stage s, returns feedback to the agent with suggestion for revision and guidance on writing the remaining parts. Judge (giving quality score, deepseek-v4-pro): given the paper title, abstract, introduction, and the real related work as target, and the final deliverables from all six schedules, scores (110 integer scale) each deliverable on coverage and factual accuracy. For each schedule listed in Table 1, we measure its quality through the judge, and its cost through the length of the deliverable, as explained in detail below. Quality. Two dimensions are scored by the judge: coverage (does the draft mention the key prior works, methods, and findings?) and factual accuracy (are citations, method names, and claims attested?). After all final deliverables are produced, for each paper p, the judge scores the deliverables produced under the six schedules. To reduce the effect of presentation order, we repeat this scoring three times, each time with randomized order of the six deliverables presented to the judge. Then the medians across the three repetitions 19 are set as the quality score for each dimension. The paper-level quality per schedule is then given by the average of the two dimensions quality scores. Cost. Human oversight cost is measured by the total length of draft content the human must read when reviewing. To obtain it, each time the LLM with human role is called we measure the length of the draft itself. The paper-level cost per schedule is then given by the sum of such lengths at the schedules oversight stages. Figure 3: Qualitative comparison of one Study 1 example, showing the final related work sections produced under four distinct oversight schedules. Each column corresponds to one schedule, and each row shows one aspect of the draft. The schedule satisfying the nonuniformity principle (Spread, with increasing gaps) performs best: it correctly identifies the Haim et al. attack as the target, keeps the text in related work tone, and captures the core non-uniqueness argument as theory. The schedule with uniform gaps (Uniform) also identifies the target and includes some core theory, but it drifts toward the method tone. The schedule with decreasing gaps (Burst-Late) partially recovers the target and the core theory but writes too late in the draft, and it retains result tone. The schedule with no oversight (Skip) performs worst: it changes target by describing new reconstruction attack and omits the core theory. Results. Table 2 reports mean quality and cost for each schedule. Figure 4 plots all six 20 schedules in quality-cost space with the Pareto hull and the optimal schedule under different values of λ. Figure 3 gives qualitative comparison for one example paper under four distinct oversight schedules. Table 2: Study 1 results: writing related work with = 40 ICLR 2026 papers. Cost is the mean paper-level oversight cost, measured by the total length of draft content read at the schedules oversight stages. Quality is the mean paper-level score, where each paper-level score averages the judge scores for coverage and factual accuracy. (110 scale). schedule is on the Pareto hull if it minimizes the loss Lλ(S) = (10 quality) + λ cost for some λ 0 among the five schedules with = 3. Schedule Cost (mean) Quality (mean SE) On Pareto hull? Skip Burst-Early Tilt-Early Spread Uniform Burst-Late 0 511 819 1122 1180 1797 3.93 0.16 4.86 0.18 4.94 0.12 5.06 0.18 5.01 0.13 5.05 0.12 The Pareto hull of study 1 comprises {Burst-Early, Spread}. Spread achieves the best quality score 5.06 in this study at the cost of 1122 tokens. These optimal schedules with non-decreasing gaps inspired the nonuniformity principle.

sections.title: 4.3 Study 2: constructing an HTML page
sections.content: This study focuses on human-AI coworking for constructing an HTML page. We present the setup, metrics, and results below, and the full implementation details of this study are in Section of the supplementary material. Setup. We construct = 10 tasks, each consisting of an initial prompt and design intent document. For each task instance, we set up three roles, agent for building the page (writing the HTML code), human for providing oversight, and judge for giving quality scores on the final pages, each realized with separate LLM or vision language model calls with temperature = 0: 21 Figure 4: Quality-cost Pareto frontier of study 1: writing related work. Summarized on = 40 ICLR 2026 papers. Each point is one oversight schedule. Error bars show 1 SE across papers. Quality (vertical axis): mean composite judge score (mean of median coverage and factual accuracy, 110 scale). Cost (horizontal axis): mean variable human read tokens per paper, intercept-removed (proxy for how much draft the human reads at each stage; grows linearly with step index t). The dashed Pareto hull connects Burst-Early and Spread, the two oversight schedules retained by lower convex-envelope extraction; Skip (cost 0) is shown for reference but excluded from the hull and the λ strip. The bottom strip shows which schedule minimizes Lλ(S) = (10 quality) + λ cost as the cost weight λ increases. Spread wins up to λ 3.3 104, beyond which the schedule Burst-Early wins. 22 Agent (building the HTML page, deepseek-v4-flash, text-only): given only the initial prompt, constructs the landing page section by section over = 10 build steps, where each step adds one major section. At any oversight stage, the agent revises the page based on human oversight. Human (providing oversight, claude-sonnet-4-6, vision): at any oversight stage S, given the design intent document as specification θ and the rendered screenshot of the HTML code produced up to stage s, returns feedback to the agent with suggestions for revision and guidance on constructing the remaining parts. Judge (giving quality score, claude-sonnet-4-6, vision): given the final rendered pages under all six schedules, scores (110 integer scale) each page on intent alignment and visual hierarchy. For each schedule listed in Table 1, we measure quality through the judge, and cost through the depth of the page build at which human oversight occurs, as explained in detail below. Quality. Two dimensions are scored by the judge: hidden intent alignment (does the page match the tone, required emphasis, constraints, and section ordering in the design intent document?) and visual hierarchy (is the layout clear, well-spaced, scannable, and effective in emphasizing calls to action?). After all final pages are produced, for each task, the judge scores the pages produced under the six schedules. To reduce the effect of presentation order, we repeat this scoring three times, each time with randomized order of the six pages presented to the judge. Then the medians across the three repetitions are set as the quality score for each dimension. The task-level quality per schedule is then given by the average of the two dimensions quality scores. Cost. Human oversight cost is directly set to at an oversight stage s. This reflects the amount of accumulated work that the human must inspect when the review occurs. The cost for an oversight schedule is then given by bC(S) = PK k=1 sk. This is fixed schedule-level proxy. Figure 5: Qualitative comparison of one example from Study 2, showing the final HTML pages produced under four distinct schedules. Each column corresponds to one schedule, and each row compares one part of the page: title, call to action, and news section. The schedule satisfying the nonuniformity principle (Tilt-Early, with increasing gaps) performs best: it uses the correct World Health Day 2026 title, preserves the required call to action framing, and includes the required news section. The schedule with uniform gaps (Uniform) is mostly correct, but it makes small error through incorrect button naming at the upper right corner. The schedule with decreasing gaps (Burst-Late) is less well aligned: its title region is too long, its call to action uses incorrect naming, and the news section is missing. The schedule with no oversight (Skip) performs worst: it has wrong campaign title, wrong call to action section, and missing news section. Results. Table 3 reports mean quality and cost for each schedule. Figure 6 plots all six schedules in quality-cost space with the Pareto hull and the optimal schedule under different values of λ. Figure 5 gives qualitative comparison for one example HTML task under four distinct oversight schedules. The Pareto hull comprises {Burst-Early, Tilt-Early}: Burst-Early has lowest cost, 24 6 and Tilt-Early has the highest quality score of 7.74. Skip is plotted as reference point but excluded from the hull, which is computed over the five oversight schedules. Tilt-Early achieves the highest quality among all oversight schedules (7.74 0.21) at moderate cost (P = 10). Uniform spends 50% more in oversight cost (P = 15) for lower quality (7.56). Burst-Late delivers the worst quality among oversight schedules (6.58) at the highest cost (P = 24), despite receiving the same number of human calls (K = 3). Table 3: Study 2 results: HTML landing-page construction over = 10 tasks. Quality is the mean page-level score, where each page-level score averages the judge scores for hidden intent alignment and visual hierarchy (110 scale). Bold denotes the highest quality scores. schedule is on the Pareto hull if it minimizes the loss Lλ(S) = (10 quality) + λ cost for some λ 0 among the five schedules with = 3. Schedule Cost (P t) Quality (mean SE) On Pareto hull? Skip Burst-Early Tilt-Early Spread Uniform Burst-Late 0 6 10 14 15 24 4.36 0.28 6.21 0.30 7.74 0.21 7.04 0.23 7.56 0.20 6.58 0.

sections.title: 4.4 Discussion of experimental results in relation to the theory
sections.content: For the experimental results shown in Figure 4 and Figure 6, we validate them with the theoretical results in Section 3. The three empirically optimal schedules have Sspread = (1, 4, 9), dspread = (1, 3, 5, 1), Stilt = (1, 3, 6), dtilt = (1, 2, 3, 4), and Searly = (1, 2, 3), dearly = (1, 1, 1, 7), respectively. All of them satisfy d0 d1 d2, which fits the main conclusion of Theorem 1. Moreover, in study 1 when λ > 3.4 104, and in study 2 when λ > 0.39, the schedule burst-early dominates. This aligns with Corollary 2.1 from Theorem 2. We also validate Algorithm 1 and Proposition 2 by deriving that under certain values of κ and η, Algorithm 1 will return Sspread = (1, 4, 9) or Stilt = (1, 3, 6) as minimizer. This result is given as follows, and the derivation details are given in Section of the supplementary 25 Figure 6: Quality-cost Pareto frontier of study 2: HTML landing-page construction over = 10 tasks. Each point is one oversight schedule; error bars show 1 SE across tasks (SE = s/ 10). Quality (vertical axis): mean of median hidden-intent alignment and visual-hierarchy judge scores (110 scale). Cost (horizontal axis): human review load t, the sum of oversight step indices (a fixed schedule constant proxying how deep into the build the reviewer must engage). The dashed Pareto hull connects Burst-Early and Tilt-Early, the two oversight schedules retained by lower convex-envelope extraction; Skip (cost 0) is shown for reference but excluded from the hull and the λ strip. The bottom strip shows which schedule minimises Lλ(S) = (10 quality) + λ cost as the cost weight λ increases. Tilt-Early wins up to λ 0.38, beyond which the schedule Burst-Early wins. 26 material. For = 10 and = 3, Algorithm 1 returns Sspread = (1, 4, 9) as minimizer whenever (cid:26) max 1 6κ, 1 4κ 2 , 1 2κ 3 , 3κ 2 (cid:27) η min (cid:26) 2 5κ, 2 3κ 2 , 3κ (cid:27) . (14) The region (14) is nonempty for 1 9 κ 4 13. For example, κ = 0.2 and η = 0.4 satisfy (14), and Algorithm 1 returns bd = (1, 3, 5, 1) and bS = (1, 4, 9). Similarly, Algorithm 1 returns dtilt = (1, 2, 3, 4) as minimizer whenever (cid:26) max 4 4κ, 4 3κ 2 , 4 2κ 3 , κ 2 (cid:27) η min (cid:26) 2κ, 5 3κ, 5 2κ 2 (cid:27) . (15) The region 15 is nonempty for 3 κ < 1. For example, κ = 0.7 and η = 1.3 satisfy (15), and Algorithm 1 returns bd = (1, 2, 3, 4), bS = (1, 3, 6). Finally, the burst-early schedule with Searly = (1, 2, 3) and dearly = (1, 1, 1, 7) is minimizer returned by Algorithm 1 whenever η > 7 2κ. This also agrees qualitatively with the empirical frontier: as λ > 3.4 104 in study 1 and λ > 0.39 in study 2, the optimal schedule is burst-early. Overall, the experimental results align with the nonuniformity principle. Across the two studies, the optimal schedules have non-decreasing gaps, which agrees with the main result of Theorem 1. The validation based on Algorithm 1 further shows that the empirically favorable schedules also arise from the proposed practical guide under appropriate parameter regions.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: We formalize human-AI coworking as process where an AI constructs deliverable and receives oversight from the human at given number of selected stages. The objective is 27 to find the best schedule that minimizes combination of the alignment loss between the final deliverable and the humans requirement, and the oversight cost. We propose the nonuniformity principle: when misalignment accumulates as the AI works and reviewing later deliverable is more costly, the optimal oversight schedule has non-decreasing gaps. That is, human oversight is better concentrated earlier and become progressively less frequent as the task proceeds. The principle is motivated and supported by empirical studies, where schedules with non-decreasing gaps achieve favorable quality-cost trade-offs. We suggest two directions for future research. First, this paper studies the timing of human oversight. broader framework could jointly determine when oversight should occur, what aspects of the intermediate deliverable should be reviewed, and how feedback should be provided. Such framework could also allow the number of oversight stages itself to be adaptive. Second, AI-for-science workflows such as hypothesis generation and experimental design present high-stakes setting where the nonuniformity principle applies directly: these tasks are long-horizon, specification-rich, and expensive to review in full. Empirical studies in scientific discovery pipelines would test the principles scope and inform the design of oversight-aware autonomous research agents.

sections.title: Use of Generative AI Tools
sections.content: During the preparation of this manuscript, the authors used AgentLab (MorphMind) to prototype the oversight scheduling pipelines in the experiments, design test cases for the algorithm, and assist with figure design. Claude Opus 4.8 (Anthropic) was used for coding assistance and language improvement. The authors reviewed and edited all outputs and take full responsibility for the content of this manuscript.
